next_best_action: Renew or replace the TLS certificate on client-api.contoso.com before it expires on March 20. This is a production-facing API gateway — expiry will break all external client integrations. Treat as P1 given 3-day window and the fact that the original provisioner has left the company.
remediation_steps: Identify the current certificate authority and certificate type (public CA, internal PKI, or Let's Encrypt)
Determine whether auto-renewal was configured or if the cert was manually provisioned
If auto-renewal exists, trigger a manual renewal cycle and verify the new cert is issued
If manual, generate a CSR, obtain a new certificate from the CA, and install it on the API gateway
Verify the new certificate is correctly installed and all client connections work without TLS errors
Configure auto-renewal and monitoring alerts to prevent recurrence

next_best_action: Investigate laptop performance degradation. Symptoms (slow performance, fan running, pop-ups about 'updating system components') are more consistent with a stuck Windows update or background Intune policy compliance than malware, especially since Defender found nothing. Check update history and system resource usage.
remediation_steps: Check Windows Update history — verify whether recent updates completed or are stuck retrying
Review Task Manager for high CPU/disk processes (likely Windows Update or Intune compliance)
If updates are stuck, run Windows Update troubleshooter or manually reset the update components
Verify Defender definitions are current and run a quick scan to confirm no threats
If the user clicked a suspicious email, forward the email to phishing@contoso.com and notify Security Operations as a precaution

If the user already clicked the link, escalate to a full security incident and initiate credential reset
